column1: Below is a detailed timeline table of the Saka-Scythian culture, beginning with the Proto-Indo-European (PIE) culture and proceeding through major migrations, events, figures, wars, prophets, tribes, and interactions with other civilizations. Dates are provided with approximate BCE values, including 876 BCE timestamps where possible.

column4: PIE culture develops in the Pontic-Caspian steppe, laying the linguistic and cultural foundation for later Indo-Iranian and Saka-Scythian peoples1.
Precursor cultures to Scythians and Saka; spread of Indo-Iranian languages1.
Kurgan burials and "animal style" art appear in the Altai and Yenisei regions1.
Genetic and archaeological evidence of eastward expansion and local adaptation; contact with Siberian and East Asian populations1.
Saka and Scythian tribes are established as major nomadic groups in the Eurasian Steppe12.
Massagetae migrate into Central Asia, expel early Scythians, triggering westward migrations and displacements (Cimmerians, Agathyrsi)1.
Saka presence attested in Tarim Basin; interactions with Zhou China possible1.
Scythians invade Western Asia, impacting Mesopotamia, Anatolia, Egypt, and Iran1.
Scythians dominate parts of the Near East, interact with Assyrians and Medes34.
Tasmola culture Saka migrate to Southern Urals, assimilate with local cultures1.
Parthians rebel against Medes, ally with Saka; Queen Zarinaea leads Saka in wars against Medes1.
30 Saka kurgans dated to 550–250 BCE found in Tian Shan region1.
Darius I defeats Massagetae/Sakā tigraxaudā, captures King Skunxa, incorporates Saka into Achaemenid Empire1.
Royal Saka burial in Kazakhstan; evidence of elite culture1.
Pazyryk burials in Altai (Ukok Plateau) show Saka elite connections; similar to Tarim mummies1.
Saka tribes migrate into Bactria, Sogdiana, Arachosia; establish Indo-Saka kingdoms5.
Indo-Saka take Arachosia from local rulers5.
Indo-Saka under Maues invade Indo-Greek territories; resistance by Indo-Greek and Saka dynasties5.
End of Indo-Greek rule, rise of Indo-Saka dominance in northwest India5.
Sarmatians, related to Saka, conquer Scythians in the west, later interact with Romans and Slavs8.
